coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention in recent years due to their interesting properties, such as photoluminescence, biocompatibility, excellent water dispersibility, as well as their potential applications in different fields. Despite these features, the complexity of their photoluminescent properties remains a subject of ongoing research and still presents unresolved questions. In this work, we investigated the preparation of various CDs from different low molecular mass starting materials containing carbon, nitrogen and/or sulfur, using hydrothermal carbonization as the preparation method. The CDs were characterized using various techniques, including TEM, FTIR, photoluminescence, UV–Vis, Raman and XPS. A detailed analysis of the initial reactions between the precursors was conducted, and our main results suggest that the optical properties of the CDs are related to fluorophores formed during the initial Michael condensation reactions between the precursors. This process produces molecular fluorescence, which plays a pivotal role in the photoluminescence properties of the nanoparticles. In addition, two of the obtained CDs were used as selective and sensitive probes for metal ions, achieving a detection limit of 0.58 μM and 0.55 μM. Finally, the findings described here can guide future works in fine-tuning the design of CDs with desired properties and different potential applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention in recent years due to their interesting properties, such as photoluminescence, biocompatibility, excellent water dispersibility, as well as their potential applications in different fields. Despite these features, the complexity of their photoluminescent properties remains a subject of ongoing research and still presents unresolved questions. In this work, we investigated the preparation of various CDs from different low molecular mass starting materials containing carbon, nitrogen and/or sulfur, using hydrothermal carbonization as the preparation method. The CDs were characterized using various techniques, including TEM, FTIR, photoluminescence, UV–Vis, Raman and XPS. A detailed analysis of the initial reactions between the precursors was conducted, and our main results suggest that the optical properties of the CDs are related to fluorophores formed during the initial Michael condensation reactions between the precursors. This process produces molecular fluorescence, which plays a pivotal role in the photoluminescence properties of the nanoparticles. In addition, two of the obtained CDs were used as selective and sensitive probes for metal ions, achieving a detection limit of 0.58 μM and 0.55 μM. Finally, the findings described here can guide future works in fine-tuning the design of CDs with desired properties and different potential applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are composed of carbonaceous materials in an approximately spherical shape, typically with diameters ranging from 2 to 5 nm, and they exhibit interesting optical properties such as UV absorption and photoluminescence [1,2]. Additionally, they are non-toxic, can be easily dispersed in aqueous media, and have a surface that can be easily functionalized to suit the desired applications [2]. These characteristics make CDs highly versatile nanomaterials with promising applications across diverse fields, such as biomedicine (bioimaging and nanomedicine) [3], optoelectronics (light-emitting diodes) [4], catalysis (pollutant degradation) [5], energy (supercapacitors and solar cells) [6,7], optics (data security and anti-counterfeiting) [8], and environmental science (sensors for metal ions and other contaminants in aqueous environments) [9].

CDs can be obtained through a myriad of methods, and generally, the strategies for obtaining these carbon nanoparticles can be classified into two types: top-down and bottom-up [2]. The former method, which was more commonly used in the nascent years of CD research, involves obtaining carbon nanoparticles by breaking down larger carbon structures such as graphite or carbon nanotubes through techniques such as electrochemical oxidation or laser ablation [2]. In recent years, the latter method has been more widely utilized, mainly due to its cost-effectiveness and the possibility to synthesize structures with higher fluorescence quantum yields (QY) [2]. Through this approach, low molecular weight compounds or macromolecules such as polymers, biomaterials, or polysaccharides can serve as precursors. Various techniques including pyrolysis, hydrothermal carbonization, or microwave-assisted methods can be employed in the process [10].

The bottom-up methods typically yield nanoparticles characterized by core-shell structures [11,12]. The core is composed of an amorphous or nanocrystalline carbogenic structure, predominantly featuring graphitic carbon layers with hybrid sp3 carbon insertions, while the shell contains functionalized oligomers and/or functional groups [12,13]. The literature describes that these oligomers and/or functional groups found on the CD shells originate from the precursor materials employed during the synthesis of carbon nanoparticles. However, a more detailed analysis of these structures formed during synthesis, which is crucial for determining the final optical properties of CDs, is often missing.

On the other hand, the research in the field of these carbon nanoparticles has experienced significant advancements, with new properties and applications being explored, such as the development of multicolor CDs [14,15], the immobilization of CDs in metal-organic frameworks to achieve fluorescent material in solid state [16], and chemiluminescence for encryption applications [17].

Currently, the preparation of CDs through bottom-up approaches, such as hydrothermal carbonization using low molar mass precursors, has been yielding CDs with higher QY values, although the photoluminescence mechanism of these nanoparticles is not yet fully understood [18]. Two main phenomena commonly discussed to explain the fluorescence mechanism in CDs are band gap-type transitions and surface defects on the nanoparticles [1,13]. The first mechanism is predominantly associated with the graphitic carbon core within sp2 domains, allowing CDs, similar to large aromatic molecules, to exhibit transitions between π and π* orbitals. The second mechanism originates from what are known as surface defects, referring to any site that lacks a perfect sp2 domain and acts as an energy trap. In practical terms, these defects are induced by functional groups present on the surface of the nanoparticles. These sites create or induce surface energy traps, which can act as capture centers for excitons. The radiation resulting from the recombination of trapped excitons induces emission, thus leading to fluorescence associated with surface states. Another significant source of fluorescence, often neglected in the literature on CDs, arises from the potential formation of fluorophores during the preparation of carbon nanoparticles, which can be incorporated within the CDs and/or on the nanoparticle shell [19,20].

The establishment of a correlation between the chemical structure of the precursors (both carbon and nitrogen sources) and the final properties of the CDs is crucial for rationalizing the development of new CDs with desired properties and potential applications. Therefore, in this study, we investigated different carbon dots derived from various precursors, incorporating carbon along with nitrogen and/or sulfur sources through hydrothermal carbonization in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Furthermore, we demonstrate through a comprehensive literature review and experimental evidence, that understanding the initial reactions, their mechanisms, and the formation of fluorophore intermediates during the synthesis of functionalized carbon nanostructures is crucial for elucidating the optical properties of CDs. The formation of fluorophores structures during the initial stages of CD formation is investigated, and their properties are correlated with those of the obtained nanoparticles. Additionally, some of the obtained CDs were utilized as sensors for metal ions.

Ammonium citrate, p-phenylenediamine (1,4-benzenediamine), thiourea, urea, and phenylthiourea were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). Succinic and maleic acids were obtained from Synth (São Paulo, Brazil). All reagents were used without further purification.

The carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal carbonization method using a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Firstly, a specific amount of an aqueous solution of the carbon source (maleic or succinic acid) was added to an aqueous solution of one of the nitrogen sources (p-phenylenediamine, ammonium citrate, thiourea, urea or phenylthiourea) to form a 1:1 M ratio of carbon to nitrogen source in the reaction mixture. After magnetic stirring, a clear and transparent solution was obtained and the material was transferred to an autoclave that was sealed and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After carbonization, the material was naturally cooled to room temperature and the obtained nanocarbons dispersions were filtered using a hydrophilic 0.22 μm membrane. Finally, the samples were dried in a vacuum oven at 100 °C for 48 h to obtain the carbon nanoparticles as a dry powder. Dialysis experiments were performed using a Sigma-Aldrich membrane with a 14 kDa cutoff.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The samples that presented QY ≥ 5 % were studied as fluorescent probes for different metal ions. Firstly, in order to study the selectivity, 50 μL of a solution containing different metal ions (1.0 × 10−2 mol L−1) was added to 2.5 mL of a CD dispersion (20 mg L−1). For stabilization purposes, the fluorescence intensity was measured 2 min after the metal addition (under magnetic stirring) into the CD suspension.

To study the sensitivity, different amounts of a dilute solution (50 μM) containing a specific metal ion were added to 2.5 mL of a 20 mg mL−1 dispersion of carbon dots. Again, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity was collected 2 min after the metal addition into the CD dispersion in order to stabilize the system.
The UV–Vis absorption spectra were acquired in a UV–Vis spectrometer (UV–Vis, Shimadzu UV-2550). The suspensions of the CDs were placed in a quartz cuvette (1.0 cm pathlength) to obtain the spectra.

The fluorescence of the samples was recorded using a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence spectrometer using a quartz cuvette with 1.0 cm of optical path. In order to calculate the quantum yield (QY) of fluorescence of the samples, the spectra of the CDs and the quinine sulfate (standard) were acquired using the same excitation wavelength (340 nm) and the following equation:

In this equation, CD is the carbon dot sample and ST is the standard. Φ is the quantum yield, S is the angular coefficient while η is the refractive index. Since the solutions of the CDs and the sulfate quinine are considerably diluted, we can consider the refractive index of both solutions ∼1.33. The QY values were calculated using five different concentrations for each CD sample.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed using an Agilent FTIR spectrometer (Cary 630). The infrared spectra were obtained in the 4000-500 cm−1 region with an attenuated total reflectance accessory using a zinc selenide (ZnSe) window.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CDs were obtained using a Tecnai G2-20 SuperTwin FEI (200 kV). After dispersion of the CDs in dilute suspensions, a single drop of the nanoparticles suspension was deposited on an ultrathin carbon film (3 nm thick) and allowed to dry at room temperature.
X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+ X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) Microprobe.

Raman spectra were recorded on a Witec alpha300 RA spectrometer using a laser beam of wavelength λ = 523 nm. One drop of the sample suspension was deposited onto a clean glass slide, and the sample was air-dried at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the different carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the bottom-up methodologies for the preparation of CDs, these nanoparticles can be obtained through various methods, including conventional hydrothermal carbonization, microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization, pyrolysis or heating reflux reaction technique [2,13]. It is known that the method used strongly influences the final properties of the obtained CDs, such as the degree of carbonization, the presence and the amount of different functional groups on the nanoparticle surface, emission wavelength, and the quantum yield of fluorescence (QY). In the literature, conventional hydrothermal carbonization (HC) in an autoclave is described as a method that can yield CDs with significantly high QY values, provided that appropriate precursors are employed [21,22]. Furthermore, this method is easy to use, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly, as it typically utilizes water as the sole solvent. Based on these characteristics, we have selected the HC method in autoclave as the experimental method to produce different CDs using various precursors with the aim of establishing structure-property relationships between the molecular precursors and the final properties of the resulting CDs.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is a crucial characteristic of CDs, as many applications of these nanoparticles, such as their use in nanosensors or bioimaging, often require a relatively high QY value. In this study, the QY values of the various samples were determined using the slope method with quinine sulfate as the standard.

Fig. S1 depicts the integrated PL intensity plotted against the absorption for different sample concentrations and the standard quinine sulfate. The QY values were calculated from the slopes of these curves and are presented in Table 1 , along with the sample names and the specific combinations of carbon and nitrogen sources used for each sample. The column in Table 1 labeled “nitrogen/sulfur source” includes precursors containing only nitrogen and those containing both nitrogen and sulfur, such as thiourea and phenylthiourea.

Using the same experimental conditions for all samples, the QY values were quite different depending on the precursors used. Specifically, the samples MAU and SUAC exhibited significantly higher QY values compared to the other obtained samples. It is also important to note that the sample MAPP is the only one that exhibited fluorescence in the yellow region of the spectrum. This is significant because it is uncommon to obtain CDs with a red-shift when using only water as a solvent during synthesis and without the use of acidic condition. Some published works have developed certain premises about the relationship between precursor composition and the final optical properties of CDs, primarily correlating the amount of the N and/or S element on the nanoparticle surface with the QY and/or with the emission fluorescence color [12,16,23]. Our results and some important data from the literature indicate that these generalizations may not be sufficient to fully explain the optical properties of the CDs [14,24,25].

With the precursors selected for this study, which involve molecules containing carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur elements, a simple analysis indicating an increase in QY with a higher percentage of N and/or S is not valid (Table S1). Elemental analysis by XPS demonstrates that there is no direct relationship between the percentage of N and/or S and the QY values. For example, considering samples prepared only with a C and N precursors, the SUAC sample, exhibited the highest QY value (28.7 %), and contains 8.42 % of N, while the SUPP sample, which presented a QY below 0.5 %, contains 12.55 % of N in its composition (Table S1). Therefore, the results suggest that a more in-depth analysis of the reactions leading to the formation of functional groups and/or fluorophores on CDs (formed in the early stages of reactions involving the chosen precursors) is crucial for understanding the optical properties of these nanoparticles. These aspects will be further discussed following the thorough characterization of the CDs samples that exhibited the highest QY values and the nanoparticle that displayed fluorescence in the yellow region of the spectrum.

Fig. 1 displays transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the samples with the highest QY values, MAU and SUAC, along with the sample exhibiting yellow fluorescence, MAPP. The images were acquired from aqueous dilute suspensions after water evaporation onto an ultra-thin carbon film. The TEM images of the samples revealed dispersed nanoparticles with nearly spherical morphology, exhibiting a relatively monodisperse size distribution.

From various images captured for each sample, it was possible to obtain the average size and size distribution of the different CDs samples. The histograms in Fig. S2, fitted with Gaussian curves, illustrate the size distribution of the carbon nanoparticles. The average particle size for the samples were determined to be 7 nm (MAPP), 2.9 nm (MAU), and 3.9 nm (SUAC). In addition, HRTEM images (Fig. S3) revealed distinct crystalline planes for the MAU sample, with interplanar distances of 0.29 nm corresponding to the (101) graphitic lattice planes [26]. In contrast, the HRTEM images of the MAPP and SUAC samples lacked discernible crystalline planes, suggesting that these CDs are predominantly amorphous.

In order to reveal the optical properties of the different CDs obtained, UV–Vis and fluorescence techniques were carried out. Fig. 2 (a–c) show the UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths for the MAPP, MAU, and SUAC samples. The insets in each figure depict photographs of the samples dispersed in water illuminated with natural light (left) and with a UV light source (right). When the samples were exposed to daylight, they showed a transparent appearance with a light brown coloration. When exposed to UV radiation (λ = 365 nm), the samples exhibited either yellow (Fig. 2a) or blue fluorescence (Fig. 2b an c).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra for the MAU and SUAC samples show absorption bands with a maximum around 320 nm due to n → π* transitions of CO bonds from different functional groups [10,27]. Interestingly, the sample prepared with p-phenylenediamine (MAPP), also exhibited a strong absorption band around 230–270 nm. This absorption band is associated with aromatic π-π* transitions of the conjugated CC bonds [22]. Besides this strong absorption band at low wavelengths, the MAPP samples also showed a shoulder/weak absorption around 350 nm, which is related to n → π* transitions of CO bonds [22].

The photoluminescence spectra of the samples (Fig. 2) were acquired under different excitation wavelengths, as indicated in each figure. The samples MAU and SUAC show only one fluorescence emission peak (wavelength range from ∼390 to ∼ 470 nm), which shift as a function of the excitation wavelength. This excitation-dependent emission can be explained by the presence of a broader distribution of diverse surface functional groups, each with distinct energy levels, on the surface of the CDs [28,29]. On the other hand, the sample MAPP exhibited two fluorescence emission peaks centered in two different regions. When this sample is irradiated with shorter wavelengths (270–330 nm), it exhibits two emission peaks: one centered at 350 nm and the other at 530 nm, with the latter falling in the yellow region of the spectrum. Additionally, when this same sample is irradiated with longer wavelengths (340–370 nm), it exhibits only the emission peak in the yellow region (∼530 nm). Furthermore, this sample exhibits a smaller excitation-dependent emission shift compared to the other samples, particularly for the second emission peak (around 530 nm). It is important to note the particular interest in CDs with red-shifted emission, especially for their potential use as fluorescent markers in biological applications [30]. Since cells and tissues naturally emit fluorescence in the blue range of the spectrum, using blue-fluorescent CDs may result in signal overlap between the nanoparticles and the intrinsic fluorescence of the tissues, which could compromise the accuracy of biomedical imaging. Thus, due to the relevance of obtaining red-shift CDs, the discussion about the preparation of this kind of carbon nanoparticles has been intense in the literature in the last few years [25,31]. In general, it is described that the presence of π conjugations plays a central role in obtaining carbon nanoparticles with these characteristics [26]. Also, it is important to mention that most studies that describe the preparation of red-shifted CDs typically involve the use of organic solvents such as dimethylformamide, ethanol or acetic acid [32]. In contrast, this study successfully obtained CDs that emit fluorescence in the yellow region of the spectrum using solely water as the solvent.

To gain a more comprehensive understanding of the photoluminescence behavior of the obtained CDs, we conducted stability experiments to examine the effects of temperature, salinity, and pH on the fluorescence intensity. The results are shown in Figs. S4, S5, and S6, respectively. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of the studied CDs remains stable across the considered range of temperature variations. Regarding salinity concentration, despite a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity for the MAPP sample with increasing KCl concentration, the overall salinity concentration had a negligible effect on the PL intensity of the studied CDs. Moreover, the samples demonstrated increased intensity values at pH levels near neutrality, accompanied by a slight decrease in intensity at higher pH levels.

An implicit question that frequently arises in the literature on CDs is why certain sources and combinations of precursors are more effective at producing CDs with notably high QY values. This question often remains unanswered due to an incomplete understanding of the primary chemical processes involved in CD formation. It is known that the main mechanism of fluorescence phenomenon in CDs is due to the presence of defects/functional groups on the nanoparticles surface that capture excitons, and therefore, understanding the formation of these functional groups is crucial for comprehending the optical characteristics of these nanoparticles. Thus, establishing a correlation between the chemical structure of the precursors (both carbon and nitrogen sources) and the final properties of the CDs is crucial for rationalizing the development of new CDs with desired properties and possible applications. These aspects can be explored and understood through a careful investigation of the reaction mechanisms, with a primary focus on the initial steps and subsequent reactions that lead to the formation of the final carbon nanoparticles.

In general, the mechanism of CD formation through the bottom-up pathway is described as occurring in various stages [12]. It begins with initial reactions that eventually lead to the formation of fluorophores, depending on the precursors employed. Generally, these initial precursors contain polymerizable functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl and double carbon bond subunits that are expected to participate in condensation and addition reaction. In fact, it has been stablished that the chemical structures of CDs can be tailored by selecting the appropriate precursors and these precursor-dependent features enable the control of the final properties of the CDs structures. For instance, condensation reactions between citric acid and amines generate fluorescent molecules. Recent research has highlighted the intrinsic relationship (atomic connectivity) between the target carbon dots and the molecular precursors, demonstrating how this connection directly influences their fluorescence properties [12]. Therefore, understanding the mechanisms of the chemical reactions involved in obtaining these intermediates, which serve as the precursors for the CDs, is crucial for gaining a deeper understanding and better control of the chemical process. The initial reactions are followed by polymerization reactions of these moieties, resulting in oligomers and subsequently crosslinked polymers. As these polymerization reactions progress, rigid entanglements are formed within the nanoparticle core, while an extended shell develops simultaneously [12]. Concurrently, carbonization and aromatization reactions occur within the dense polymeric core. As reactions like dehydration, decarboxylation and deamination occur, the relative amount of carbon within the nanoparticle core increases. Meanwhile, the shell is primarily composed of the main functional groups and/or fluorophores formed from the precursor molecules [15].

To understand the initial formation of fluorophores and functional groups on CDs, we performed a detailed analysis of the reaction mechanism involving the precursors used in CD synthesis. These reactions directly yield intermediates that serve as the precursors for the CD backbone, fluorophore units, and functional groups on the nanoparticles surface. The discussion will primarily focus on the CDs with the highest QY values (MAU and SUAC), as well as the MAPP sample, which exhibited yellow fluorescence.

In a general evaluation of our results (Table 1), it is noticeable that maleic acid and succinic acid are efficient sources for synthesizing CDs, depending on the nitrogen source. In fact, only one of the carbon sources used is an alpha, beta-unsaturated carboxylic acid, such as maleic acid. However, succinic acid undergoes thermal decomposition, also generating the corresponding alpha, beta-unsaturated carboxylic acid. In this way, under our HC conditions and in the presence of an appropriate nitrogen source (urea, thiourea, or phenylthiourea, which also serves as a sulfur source), both carbon sources, maleic and succinic acid, react through a Michael reaction or, more specifically, an aza-Michael reaction/condensation [33]. Nevertheless, in general, these Michael reactions can be easily carried out under mild conditions with diester derivatives and organic amines, even at room or moderate temperatures [34]. Indeed, in the reaction between maleic acid and p-phenylenediamine (MAPP), only the Michael reaction occurs, as will be discussed in detail later. However, this reaction does not occur using succinic acid with p-phenylenediamine, and our data supports this lack of reactivity. In the cases of MAU, MAT, MAPT, SUU, SUT, and SUPT, where the nitrogen source is urea or its thio-derivatives, high temperatures lead to their thermal decomposition [35], generating ammonia or its isocyanic or phenylthiocyanic analogues, which may exhibit higher reactivity, or the thiocyanic derivative (H–S–CN), which has lower reactivity. Ammonia reacts with the unsaturated acid through a Michael reaction, forming aspartic, diaspaspartic, and even polyaspartic acids. These compounds can then react with the isocyanic acid derivative, depending on its reactivity, which leads to an intermediate that undergoes a cyclization reaction. This reaction may produce fluorescent heterocyclic moieties, which will be discussed in detail later.

For the MAPP sample, Scheme 1 a illustrates the initial reaction between para-phenylenediamine (1) and maleic acid (2) through a well-documented Michael-type addition in an aqueous environment [36]. This process yields two products: the N-(4-aminophenyl)aspartic (1–2.A) and N-[4-(1,2-Dicarboxyethyl)amino]phenylaspartic acids (1–2.B), depending on the molar ratio between the reagents (Scheme 1a). Subsequently, a cyclization reaction takes place with acid catalysis and heating, forming 1,10-dioxo-4,7-phenanthroline-3,8-dicarboxylic acids with high thermal stability (>320 °C) [37]. Therefore, after heating (180 °C/8h) using conventional hydrothermal carbonization (HC), the intermediate products 1.2-A and 1.2-B (Scheme 1a) are obtained. The di-substituted derivative 1.2-B may yield 1,2,3,4,7,8,9,10-octahydro-1,10-dioxo-4,7-phenanthroline-3,8-dicarboxylic acid 1.2-B.2 and its monocyclic analogue 1.2-B.1 (Scheme 1b).

The analysis of the infrared spectrum of the MAPP sample (Fig. 3 ) [38,39], reveals vibrational bands corresponding to the functional groups of the acyclic aspartic products 1–2.A and 1–2.B, as well as the cyclic derivatives 1.2-B.1 and 1.2-B.2 [40].

The bands that appeared around 3200-3300 cm−1 are characteristics of –NH and-OH stretching vibrations [33,34]. Additionally, the band at 1700 cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching of aryl ketones, while the bands at 1650, 1550, and 1380 cm−1 display characteristic signals from quinolone/pyridine heterocyclic systems. The signal at 1610 cm−1 is attributed to CO conjugated with aromatic structures [33]. Finally, the signals at 1505 and 820 cm−1 can be attributed to in-plane N–H bending vibration of secondary amines and out of plane deformation vibrations of aromatic groups, respectively [33].

In addition to the IV analyses, XPS measurements were also conducted on the samples. Fig. S7 exhibited the full scale XPS spectra acquired for MAPP, MAU, and SUAC samples. The XPS for the MAPP sample consist mainly of C, N and O (Fig. S7 and Table S1). The high resolution XPS (Figure S8 a-c) shows the three peaks of C1s that could be attributed to CC (283.2 eV), C–N (284.1 eV) and CO of carboxylic (286.6 eV) [41,42]. The N1s spectrum shows two peaks at 397.9 eV and 398.9 eV corresponding to pyridinic N and amine, respectively [37]. The O1s spectrum shows the presence of CO (530.2 eV) and C–OH (531.5 eV) [36]. These features indicate the presence of the moieties shown in Scheme 1, revealing the important role of the initial reactions in the final structure of the CDs.

The cyclic derivatives 1.2-B.1 and 1.2-B.2 (Scheme 1b) share structural similarities with quinoline-like structures such as kynurenine yellow which exhibits fluorescence in the yellowish range of the spectrum [43]. Thus, the yellowish fluorescence color observed in this sample is an additional indication of the presence of these moieties.

In addition to characterization techniques such as FTIR and XPS analysis, chemical reactivity can also be assessed using thermogravimetry, as demonstrated by Pohlmann in the high-temperature carbonization and aromatization of biomass [44]. This process leads to florescent materials with a decrease in the volatile matter and an increase in carbon and nitrogen content. Thus, the chemical interaction between reagents 1 and 2 (Scheme 1a) can also be investigated by thermal analysis. Initially, it is observed that 1 and 2 melt during the HC process, since their respective melting points are 138 °C and 180 °C (Figs. S9a and S9b). Subsequently, high physicochemical interactions are induced, as described by Rutkauskas and Berenesvicius [31], leading to the initial formation of 1–2.A and 1–2.B, which exhibit melting points exceeding 200 °C. Moreover, the remaining reagents may decompose at the elevated temperatures of the HC process, as shown in Fig. S9c. The thermogravimetry analysis of a heated physical mixture of compounds 1 and 2 (Fig. S9c) shows evidence of their chemical interaction, producing novel thermal events around 200–220 °C, likely linked to the formation of 1–2.A and 1–2.B. Subsequent thermal events at 420 °C and 550 °C result in the production of highly stable materials until 600 °C. These final products might be related to the decarboxylation and aromatization of 1.2-B.1 and 1.2-B.2. However, it's worth noting that at 180 °C, the HC process has limited ability to promote aromatization, which may result in a CD with low QY value.

When analyzing the reactions involved in the formation of the MAU sample, it is important to note that organic amines, urea, and ammonia are frequently utilized as source materials in the synthesis of polymers and even carbon dots [45]. In this context, substituting p-phenylenediamine for urea in the reaction with maleic acid (yielding the MAU sample) improves the chemical reactivity compared to the previous reaction. As is well-known, urea melts at 135 °C and decompose [46], yielding ammonia (3a) and isocyanic acid (HNCO, 3b), as shown in Scheme 2 . Isocyanic acid acts as a carbamoylating and dehydrating agent, enhancing the chemical reactivity of the intermediate products, thus favoring the synthesis of aspartic acid and its subsequent polymerization. Since 2002, it has been well established that malic, maleic, and fumaric acids can react with urea or organic and inorganic ammonium salts, yielding polyaspartic acid [47,48]. It is noteworthy that very recently polyaspartic acid (PAA) surface-modified blue luminescent carbon quantum dots were prepared using the hydrothermal approach [49].

Scheme 2a illustrates the production of aspartic acid (2–3.A) as a product from the Michael addition reaction of 2, which can generate alpha-asparagylaspartic acid (2–3.B), as first reported by Emil Fischer and Ernst Koenigs in 1907 [50]. It can also be cited that urea (3) provides a highly polar reaction medium which facilitates several reactions with dicarboxylic acids due to hydrogen bond effects [51]. Various studies have explored these reactions, with the earliest being the research conducted by Nyc and Mitchell in 1947 [52]. The research group demonstrated that orotic acid analogues can be prepared from the reaction of aspartic acid with urea. Therefore, polyaspartic acid (2–3.C) can also be obtained, as illustrated in Scheme 2a. In turn, ammonia (3a) or isocyanic acid (3b) then engage in their well-known chemical interactions with the aspartic units, yielding other reactive intermediates. These intermediates can undergo cyclization reactions, leading to the formation of orotic derivatives (2–3.D1 and 2–3.D2), as depicted in Scheme 2b. It can be cited that the diketo-intermediate orotic acid tautomer 2–3.D1 undergoes enolization to its corresponding dienol-dihydroxy-pyrimidine form 2–3.D2, particularly in highly polar and acidic environments. This transformation has been investigated both theoretically and spectroscopically [53].

The infrared spectrum of the MAU sample reveals a large band vibration around 3000 cm−1, corresponding to O–H and N–H stretching. Additionally, signals at 1555 cm−1, 1380 cm−1 and 850 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CN and CC bonds within the pyrimidine structure [33,34].

The XPS for this sample reveals the presence of C, N and O (Fig. S7 and Table S1). The high resolution XPS (Figure S8 d-f) shows the presence of four peaks of C1s which is attributed to CC and CN (284.2 eV), C–O/C–N (285.5 eV), CO (287.35 eV), and O–CO (288.26 eV) [36,37]. The N1s spectrum shows peaks at 400.2 eV, corresponding to amine/amide and 398.5 eV, related to N pyridinic structures [54]. The O1s spectrum also shows the presence of CO of carboxylic (530.0 eV) and C–O (531.1 eV) [36].

Thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. S10) shows a direct chemical interaction between maleic acid (2) and urea (3), although both compounds must melt during the HC process. It is clearly noticed that heating a physical mixture of compounds 2 and 3 reveals profiles entirely different from those of the isolated compounds. This was expected, as it has been previously noted that urea provides a highly polar reaction medium when combined with dicarboxylic acids [46]. It can be observed that urea decomposes to generate cyanuric acid and ammonia [41]. At 180 °C, other chemical processes may also take place, with suggested products from Michael addition and cyclization being preferentially formed [55], rather than other secondary and polymerization reactions involving only urea [30]. Heating the physical mixture of compounds 2 and 3 (Fig. S10) reveals a unique thermal event at 140 °C, which occurs at a lower temperature than that of the isolated reagents. This may be due to the chemical interaction between the two reagents, as well as the formation of more thermally stable fluorescent moieties and carbonaceous products that only decompose at around 600 °C. Thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. S10) may also corroborate the chemical composition of the products obtained from the HC process, as both pyrimidine [56] and orotic acid [57] exhibit high thermal stability, with decomposition occurring at temperatures above 250–300 °C and 280–355 °C, respectively.

The fluorescence of pyrimidines (such as orotic acid) in the blue region has been known since 1966 [58], and as expected, these heterocyclic compounds have garnered great attention as building blocks for the synthesis of π-conjugated materials [59]. Notably, dipyrimidines exhibit unique fluorescence properties and polypyrimidines have been also investigated with intense blue fluorescence [60]. Besides, it is important to highlight the significance of the amide functional group (NH–CO) within the structure of the CDs. This functional group assumes particular importance due to its implication in high conjugation between the interconnected molecular components. The mutual co-planarity and conjugation within the molecular scaffold of the CD is extremely important, since in a π-conjugated molecule or macromolecule, the torsional conformation between aromatic units plays a pivotal role in determining the overall molecular shape, as well as optical and electronic properties. In a coplanar conjugated system, coherent conjugation along the backbone increases the intramolecular charge transport and favors minimal reorganization energy during charge transport. These effects collectively contribute to an increased in quantum yield of fluorescence. Furthermore, the adoption of a coplanar configuration facilitates close intermolecular packing in the solid state. This arrangement is anticipated to yield strong intermolecular electronic coupling and an extended exciton diffusion length [61].

Thus, the CDs obtained from maleic acid and urea (MAU), exhibited highly fluorescent and thermally stable pyrimidinyl fluorescent moieties (an aspartic acid derivative), attached to the carbon structure via an amide link. The precursors used in the HC method produced CDs with relatively high fluorescence (QY = 21.3 %), as corroborated by comparative thermal analysis conducted on the isolated starting materials and their mixture. Notably, the pyrimidyl-aspartic acid precursor readily transformed into CDs as the final product.

Considering the sample SUAC (obtained from succinic acid and ammonium citrate), the thermal behavior of ammonium salts has been known since 1964. In the case of ammonium citrate (5, Scheme 3 ), its thermal decomposition begins around 150 °C in an endothermic process, with the maximum rate occurring at 195 °C. Complete oxidation of carbon residue is observed only above 800 °C. Additionally, carboxylic ammonium salts typically yield the corresponding carboxylic acids and ammonia [62].

Furthermore, when ammonium citrate (5) is heated in an aqueous solution at 180 °C under hydrothermal conditions, it may produce itaconic acid and excess ammonia in a 3:1 M ratio. This chemical process is similar to a Michael condensation (Scheme 3a) as previously described, and the resultant compound is identified as 2-(aminomethyl)succinic acid (6) [63]. However, another reagent, the 5-amino-carboxylic derivative 7, needs to react with this precursor to undergo a subsequent cyclization reaction (Scheme 3b). This reaction might be similar to the one reported by Gao et al., involving the reaction between citric acid and N-acetylcysteine [16].

Table 1 shows another reaction for the production of CDs involving maleic acid (2) and ammonium citrate (MAAC sample). However, this reaction was not very efficient because the key precursor, compound 7, was not formed, leading to a QY of 7.8 %. On the other hand, succinic acid can also react with the evolved ammonia to yield succinimide (7a), a well-known reaction dating back to 1894 [64]. Furthermore, 7a can undergo another reaction with ammonia to yield aspartic acid [65]. This aspartic acid can polymerize as described above in Schemes 2, potentially exhibiting even higher efficiency [42,43]. Given that ammonium citrate (5) was the source of nitrogen, it was hypothesized that the presence of succinic acid could facilitate a more efficient Michael addition reaction. This reaction, in turn, led to the generation of CDs with higher fluorescence (QY = 28.7 %).

It is suggested that the final fluorescent product (9) is obtained as a result of the initial amide condensation between 2-(aminomethyl)succinic acid (7) and diaspartic acid (2–3.B1) (Scheme 3b), as cited above and demonstrated by Gao et al. This process yields the intermediate (8), which undergoes an enol-carboxylic intramolecular cyclization [16]. Then, a second cyclization occurs, generating a 6-oxo-5,6-dihydro-2H-pyrido[2,3-d][1,3]oxazine bicyclic heterocyclic system (9) (Scheme 3b).

It should be pointed out that Sayed and Hassan [66] observed that 7-thienyl-pyrido [2,3-d]pyrimidin-4(3H)-ones, which shares quite similarities to the compounds obtained here, have shown blue fluorescence with maximum fluorescence wavelengths at 427–482 nm. It is also important to note that the scientific literature reports the production of highly photoluminescent heterobicyclic ring-fused pyridones when using similar reactions for the synthesis of CDs via the bottom-up hydrothermal method, utilizing citric acid as the precursor [67,68].

The FTIR spectrum of the SUAC sample reveals bands characteristics of OH and –NH stretching around 3200 cm−1 and CO stretching of carboxylic groups at 1700 cm−1. Furthermore, the band at 1650 cm−1 is assigned to CO vibrations of amide, whereas the peaks at 1550 and 1400 cm−1 are attributed to the in plane vibration of CC and CN bonds found in pyridine structure [33,34]. Finally, the band at ∼1170 cm−1 is assigned to the presence of C–O–C stretching bonds of oxazines [69].

The XPS spectrum of the SUAC sample exhibited the presence of C, N, and O. (Fig. S7 and Table S1). The high resolution XPS (Figure S8, g-i) reveals the presence of three peaks of C1S at 285.1 eV, 285.7 eV and 288.6 eV, which are related to CC/CN, C–N and O–CO, respectively [36]. The N1S spectrum shows two peaks at 400.1 eV and 401.7 eV corresponding to pyridinic and amine [37]. The O1S spectrum reveals the presence of CO (531.9 eV) and C–O (533.4 eV) [36].

The thermogravimetric investigation (Fig. S11) of the physical mixture of succinic acid (7) and ammonium citrate (5) reveals a distinct thermal behavior compared to the thermograms of the isolated reagents. While succinic acid (7) undergoes an isolated thermal decomposition event around 200 °C, and ammonium citrate (5) exhibits three decomposition events due to its breakdown into ammonia and methylidene succinic acid (4), the physical mixture of succinic acid (7) and ammonium citrate (5) presents a broad decomposition curve ranging from 170 °C to 245 °C. This behavior might be attributed to several Michael condensation reactions involving the formed ammonia with itaconic acid and succinic acid, leading to the formation of stable products that decompose only around 600 °C.

In addition, we carried out dialysis experiments on the MAPP, MAU, and SUAC samples to determine whether the fluorophores formed during synthesis contribute to the fluorescence from the carbon nanostructures (chemically bound to the CDs), or if these fluorophores are only present in the solution. To achieve this, the dialysis experiments were conducted to remove small molecular impurities and/or fluorophores in solution using an appropriate membrane (cut off = 14 kDa) [70]. After conducting dialysis for 24 h (with three water exchanges), we obtained the fluorescence curves of the as-synthesized material (without dialysis), the retentate, and the dialysate. The results are shown in Figs. S12–S14. Given that we used relatively large dialysis membrane pores (14 kDa) and performed multiple water exchanges, it is expected that the small molecular impurities and fluorophores were removed from the membrane. The obtained fluorescence curves show that the main characteristics of the fluorescence profiles - comparing the retentate, dialysate, and the as-synthesized material - are only slightly affected by dialysis, suggesting that at least some of the fluorescent emission centers remain in the retentate. Thus, the persistence of similar emission curve characteristics indicates that the emission-related groups, as identified by FTIR and XPS, are formed and chemically bound to the surface of the CDs.

Furthermore, the comparison of the QYs for the as-synthesized and retentate portions of the samples (Fig. S15) reveals that the MAPP and SUAC samples were minimally affected by dialysis, while the MAU sample showed a decrease in its QY. This reduction in the QY of the MAU sample suggests that a significant portion of its fluorescence originates from fluorophores in solution. In contrast, for the MAPP and SUAC samples, which underwent the same dialysis process, the QY was not similarly affected. This result, along with the fluorescence curve characteristics, suggests that the fluorophores responsible for the fluorescence in the MAPP and SUAC samples are chemically bound to the CDs and are therefore retained in the membrane.

In this way, considering all three samples, the fluorescence is influenced by the formed fluorophores, with a significant portion of the fluorescence intensity in the MAPP and SUAC samples originating from fluorophores that are part of the nanoparticle structure, as they were retained in the membrane. Additionally, even with a significant loss in QY for the MAU sample, the QY value in the retentate is approximately 25 % of the original value for this sample, indicating that at least some of the fluorophores are retained in the structure of the CDs.

Overall, considering the synthesis of the nanoparticles, the analysis of these chemical and reactivity data indicates that a more efficient Michael reaction leads to an increased production of fluorescent precursors and a higher QY in the final materials. From a chemical reactivity perspective, it can be proposed that the initial Michael condensation between the carbon and nitrogen sources used is the primary and most important reaction for producing highly fluorescent heterocyclic intermediates. These intermediates then undergo a suitable cyclization reaction and subsequent thermal carbonization to yield CDs with high QYs. In this way, in the MAPP sample, only the initial Michael addition occurs, and the sample exhibits low fluorescence (PL emission around 550 nm). For the sample MAU, the Michael condensation and subsequent cyclization reactions to obtain pyrimidines are quite efficient. These reactions also produce fluorescent materials that emit in the 400–450 nm range, with higher fluorescence due to increased conjugation from the phenylimine group.

Notably, ammonium citrate serves as both a carbon and nitrogen source, and its thermal decomposition yields an unsaturated acid and a substantial amount of ammonia. The SUAC sample yielded the best result, as the combination of succinic acid and ammonium citrate enables a double Michael condensation. This process simultaneously produces 2-(aminomethyl)succinic acid (7) and aspartic acid derivatives that react between them to yield the 5-amino-carboxylic derivative 8, which serves as a suitable intermediate for the subsequent cyclization to obtain the 6-oxo-5,6-dihydro-2H-pyrido[2,3-d][1,3]oxazine bicyclic heterocyclic system (10), with the highest fluorescence emission at 400–475 nm. In the MAAC sample, the Michael reaction of ammonia from ammonium citrate with maleic acid can produce aspartic derivatives, as well as the precursor 7 and subsequent intermediates, although these are formed in lower yields. In the SUT sample, the generation of ammonia from urea is minimal, leading to reduced yields of aspartic acid and subsequent steps.

As previously mentioned, thiourea is not a good nitrogen source because its decomposition forms thiocyanic acid, which has low reactivity. As a result, the samples MAT and SUT produce materials with low fluorescence.

In addition to the previously mentioned Michael reaction, it is known that organic materials, particularly those derived from citric acid, may undergo a graphitization process that forms small molecular fluorophores and also aromatic domains, as noted by Stolarczyk et al. [71]. In fact, our materials with best results in terms of QY (MAU and SUAC) formed aspartic acid and its corresponding oligomers and polymers. It is noteworthy that CDs formed from aspartic acid have already been described in the literature; however, they were obtained at much higher temperatures and with much longer reaction times [72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman spectra of the prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S16 displays the Raman spectra of the MAU, MAPP, and SUAC samples. Across all spectra, two prominent peaks are observed around 1355 cm−1 and 1579 cm−1. The first peak corresponds to the D band, primarily indicative of disordered carbon domains, reflecting the extent of defects and functionalization. The G band, on the other hand, is associated with more structured regions of sp2 graphitized carbon [73]. In addition, in carbon dots, the ID/IG ratio serves as a valuable metric for estimating the degree of graphitization within their internal structure. It is expected that CDs exhibiting a higher degree of carbonization will also display a more pronounced graphitic structure. Since the intensity of the G band is associated with the graphitic structure, a lower ID/IG value indicates a higher degree of graphitization in the nanoparticles. The ID/IG values obtained for the MAPP, MAU, and SUAC samples are 1.03, 0.98, and 0.85, respectively, indicating that SUAC sample presented the highest degree of graphitization. These results suggest a synergistic effect between the surface states (from surface functional groups or fluorophores) and the graphitic carbon core of the CDs [74].

Innocenzi, Malfatti, and Ren noted that the carbonization temperature around 150–200 °C is a key factor for controlling the growth and maximizing the QY of carbon dots [14]. Furthermore, the same paper highlights that nitrogen doping significantly enhances QY and suggests that primary amines may be more effective than secondary and tertiary amines. This could be due to the higher reactivity and lower steric hindrance in reactions involving primary amines, as observed in this present study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as selective probes for metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: All the obtained samples that resulted in QY values > 5 % (Table 1) were studied as sensors for different metal ions. Initially, a selectivity study was carried out through the fluorescence quenching effect in the presence of different metal ions (Zn2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+). The measurements of integrated PL intensities were recorded 2 min after the addition of 50 μL of each metal ion (at concentration of 0.01 mol L−1) into 2.5 mL of each of the CDs suspensions (2 mg L−1). Among the studied samples (those with QY> 5 %), the CDs obtained from maleic acid and thiourea (MAT) and those prepared from succinic acid and ammonium citrate (SUAC) showed considerable selectivity towards Fe3+ ions. The results for the samples MAT and SUAC in the presence of different metal ions are shown in Fig. 4 a and b, respectively. It is clear from Fig. 4 that Fe3+ ions exerted a significantly more pronounced effect on fluorescence quenching, compared to other cations, in both the MAT and SUAC samples. In the MAT sample, the presence of ferric ions resulted in a 77 % decrease in intensity, whereas a more substantial photoluminescence quenching (approximately 85 %) was observed in the presence of Fe3+ ions for the SUAC sample. The results from both samples show that the obtained CDs can be used as selective sensors for ferric ions in aqueous solutions.

The quenching mechanism in carbon dots by metal ions can be described as a result of a coordination of the metal ion with the nanoparticles, and the strength of the interaction depends on the CDs surface chemistry [75]. The surface of the CDs rich in oxygenated and nitrogen functional groups, such as carboxyl and amino, generate a strong interaction between Fe3+ ions and the surface of the nanoparticle leading to the coordination [76]. Once the coordination takes place, the nanoparticles act as electron donors to the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+, which leads to a non-radiative electron-hole recombination, thereby inducing significant fluorescence quenching [79].

To examine the sensitivity of the CDs to the presence of ferric ions and determine the limit of detection (LOD), an experiment recording the fluorescence intensity as a function of the concentration of the metallic ion was conducted. Various volumes of a Fe3+ ion solution (50 μM) were added into the CDs aqueous suspensions (0.02 mg mL−1) and the PL emission intensities were recorded 2 min after the metal ion addition, under magnetic stirring. The fluorescence spectra obtained demonstrate a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity with the addition of the metal ion solution for both the MAT and SUAC samples (Figs. S17a and b).

Fig. S18 show the integrated photoluminescence intensity as a function of the concentration of ferric ions for the samples MAT and SUAC, respectively. For both samples, the integrated PL intensity behavior is linear (with R2 above 0.99) from 0 to 30 μM of Fe3+, clearly demonstrating a linear reduction in the photoluminescence intensity as a function of ferric ion concentration.

The Stern-Volmer equation can be used to describe the PL quenching behavior:

In this equation, F
o and F are the photoluminescence in the absence and in the presence of the metal ion, respectively, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant.

Fig. 5 depicts Stern-Volmer plots for MAT (a) and SUAC (b) samples as probes in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. A good linearity (with R2 above 0.98) was observed over a relatively wide concentration range (from 0 to 30 μM) for both samples.

To calculate the limit of detection for ferric ions, the equation LOD = 3σ/S was employed. In this equation, σ represents the standard deviation, and S denotes the slope of the curve. The LOD values calculated for both samples were 0.58 μM and 0.55 μM for the MAT and SUAC samples, respectively. These results indicate that the obtained CDs are not only selective probes towards Fe3+ but also sensitive detectors for ferric ions at relative low concentration in aqueous solution.

The concentration of ferric ions in drinking water is limited to 5.36 μM (World Health Organization, WHO), suggesting that the results obtained in this study are significantly lower than the maximum allowable concentration for these ions. Therefore, the methods and materials employed here could potentially be used to determine the concentration of these ions in aqueous environments.

To elucidate the quenching mechanism of the samples in the presence of Fe3+ ions, a study with different concentrations of ferric ions at different temperatures was carried out. In a dynamic mechanism, an increase in the quenching effect with temperature is expected due to stronger collisions between the quencher and the fluorophore. On the other hand, in a static mechanism, no temperature interference in the quenching effect is expected, or a decrease in the quenching effect with temperature may be observed [77,78].

Fig. S19 exhibit the Stern-Volmer plots for the MAT and SUAC samples at various concentrations of Fe3+ ions and at different temperatures. The MAT sample showed a decrease in the quenching effect with temperature, while the SUAC sample showed no temperature-dependent quenching effect, suggesting a static quenching mechanism for both samples.

The results obtained from the MAT and SUAC samples demonstrate the potential of the synthesized carbon nanoparticles to be used as selective and sensitive probes for detecting Fe3+ ions in aqueous environments. Furthermore, these nanoprobes can be easily prepared using a simple, low cost, environmentally friendly, and one-step method.

In summary, in this work, we correlated the characteristics of the fluorophores formed during the initial reactions among the molecular precursors with the ultimate properties of the carbon nanoparticles obtained. The results obtained here suggest that understanding the primary reactions among the precursors can provide a valuable approach for fine-tuning the final properties of the CDs.

In summary, in this study we obtained various CDs from different low molecular weight precursors using HC method. The results indicate that the QY values of carbon dots should not be directly attributed solely to the amount of nitrogen and/or sulfur on the nanoparticles. Instead, our findings suggest that the initial reactions between the molecular precursors, which can form specific intermediates to produce efficient fluorophores, are crucial for understanding the optical characteristics of these nanoparticles. The expected moieties and functional groups were detected through FTIR and XPS in the final CDs formed, indicating that these initial reactions play a fundamental role in establishing the final properties of the CDs.

For the precursors used in this work, a more efficient Michael condensation reaction between the carbon and nitrogen sources leads to a higher QY in the resulting nanoparticles. For example, in the SUAC sample, which achieved the highest QY value, the combination of succinic acid and ammonium citrate enabled an effective double Michael condensation reaction. This emphasizes the importance of the initial reactions between precursors to form carbon nanoparticles with higher QY values.

The CDs synthesized from precursors containing aromatic groups exhibited UV absorption linked to aromatic π-π* transitions, while the other CDs exhibited only an absorption band around 320 nm, attributed to n→π* transitions. Additionally, two of the prepared samples, MAT and SUAC, exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ ions, with LOD values of 0.58 μM and 0.55 μM, respectively.

Finally, given that the optical characteristics of CDs, such as fluorescence quantum yield and emission wavelength, are crucial for the myriads of applications of these nanoparticles, we believe this work can pave the way for a deeper understanding of the formation, growth and development of specific optical properties in these carbon entities.
